raw_text: 1 ITEM NO.56 COURT NO.11 SECTION XIV S U P R E M E C O U R T O F I N D I A RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Civil) No(s).196492009 (From the judgement and order dated 22.7.2009 in C.W.P. No.8572009 of The HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI) DIRECTORATE OF ENFORCEMENT Petitioner(s) VERSUS ARUN KUMAR AGRAWAL  ORS. Respondent(s) (With prayer for interim relief and office report) Date: 09072010 This Petition was called on for hearing today. CORAM : HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE G.S. SINGHVI HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ASOK KUMAR GANGULY For Petitioner(s) Mr. Gopal Subramanium,S.G. Mr. Rajshekhar Rao, Adv. Mr. Sreekumar, Adv. Mr.Senthil Jagadeesan,Adv. For Respondent(s) Mr. Prashant Bhushan,Adv. Mr. Rajiv Nanda, Adv. Mr. B.K. Prasad, Adv. Mr. Kamaldeep Dayal, Adv. Mr. Siddhartha Chowdhury, Adv. 41 2 O R D E R UPON hearing counsel the Court made the following This petition is directed against order dated 22.7.2009 passed by the learned Single Judge of Delhi High Court, paragraph 11 of which reads thus: "CIC is yet to decide the question whether the information sought for is covered by Section 24(1) of the Act, whether first proviso applies and exceptions can be claimed under Section 8(1) of th e Act. Impugned order dated 29th December, 2008 makes a general observation on the basis of allegations made by the respondent No. 1 in the appeal and observes that allegations of corruption have been made. No final and determinative finding has been given by CIC. It is open to the petitioner to produce the original files and then press that the conditions mentioned in proviso to Section 24(1) of the Act are not satisfied in this case and thus provisions of Section 8(1) of the Act are not required to be examined. Dr. Arun Kumar Agrawal has contended that Mr. Virendera Dayal was not appointed by the Directorate of Enforcement and Section 24(1) of the Act is not applicable, even if the report is recently with the said Directorate. These aspects have not been decided by the CIC. It will not be appropriate for this Court to control the proceedings and flexibility and lactitude has to be allowed. The impugned orders can hardly be categorised as adverse orders against the Directorate of Enforcement." We have heard learned counsel for the parties and perused the records. In our view, the impugned order does not suffer from any patent legal infirmity requiring interference under Article 136 of the Constitution. The special leave petition is accordingly dismissed. However, it is made clear that the parties shall be entitled to make all legally permissible submissions before the Central Information Commissioner. (A.D. Sharma) (Phoolan Wati Arora) Court Master Court Master 42
facts: Facts not clearly identifiable in document.
issues: whether the information sought for is covered by section 24(1) of the act, whether first proviso applies and exceptions can be claimed under section 8(1) of th e act. impugned order dated 29th december, 2008 makes a general observation on the basis of allegations made by the respondent no. 1 in the appeal and observes that allegations of corruption have been made. no final and determinative findin

debate_transcript.speaker: Plaintiff Lawyer
debate_transcript.argument:
Honorable Court, I stand before you today to represent the plaintiff in this matter, seeking to establish the respondent's liability and claim the information sought under the Right to Information Act, 2005. The impugned order dated 29th December, 2008, makes a general observation regarding allegations of corruption, but fails to provide a final and determinative finding. I strongly argue that the information sought is not covered by Section 24(1) of the Act, as the respondent has not provided any concrete evidence to support their claim.

Furthermore, I submit that the first proviso to Section 24(1) is not applicable in this case, as the allegations made by the respondent are unsubstantiated and lack merit. The respondent's attempt to claim exceptions under Section 8(1) of the Act is also misplaced, as the information sought is not sensitive or confidential in nature. I rely on the judgment of the Hon'ble Supreme Court in the case of R.K. Jain vs. Union of India, which held that the Right to Information Act is a powerful tool for promoting transparency and accountability in governance.

I urge the Court to consider the petitioner's right to information, as enshrined under Article 19(1)(a) of the Constitution, and to exercise its discretion in favor of the petitioner. I shall demonstrate, through the course of my arguments, that the respondent's actions are unjustified and that the petitioner is entitled to the information sought. I request the Court to allow me to proceed with my arguments and present evidence in support of the plaintiff's position.

debate_transcript.speaker: Defendant Lawyer
debate_transcript.argument:
Your Honor, I strongly argue that the information sought by the appellant is indeed covered by Section 24(1) of the Act, which exempts certain organizations from disclosing information. The first proviso to this section clearly applies in this case, as the allegations made by the respondent no. 1 are unsubstantiated and lack concrete evidence. 

I challenge the plaintiff's claims by citing the landmark judgment of the Supreme Court in the case of R.K. Jain vs. Union of India, which held that the exemptions under Section 8(1) of the Act are not absolute and must be interpreted in a manner that balances the right to information with the need to protect sensitive information. In this case, the allegations of corruption are mere allegations and do not constitute a final and determinative finding, as required by the Act.

I also question the credibility of the evidence presented by the respondent no. 1, which is based on unverified allegations. The impugned order dated 29th December, 2008, makes a general observation without providing any concrete evidence to support the claims. I rely on the principle established in the case of Canara Bank vs. V.K. Awasthi, which held that unverified allegations cannot be the basis for making adverse inferences against a party. Therefore, I urge the court to exercise caution and consider the lack of concrete evidence before making any findings against my client.

debate_transcript.speaker: Plaintiff Lawyer
debate_transcript.argument:
Honorable Court, I stand before you today to argue in favor of the petitioner, seeking to establish that the information sought is not exempt under Section 24(1) of the Act. The impugned order dated 29th December, 2008, makes a general observation based on allegations of corruption, but fails to provide a final and determinative finding. This lack of conclusive evidence renders the respondent's claim of exemption under Section 8(1) of the Act untenable.

I would like to cite the landmark judgment of the Supreme Court in the case of S.P. Gupta vs. President of India, (1981) Supp SC 87, which emphasizes the importance of transparency and accountability in governance. The Court held that the right to information is a fundamental right, and any exemptions must be narrowly construed.

In the present case, the respondent has failed to demonstrate how the disclosure of the sought information would cause harm to the public interest. The first proviso to Section 24(1) of the Act does not apply, as there is no evidence to suggest that the information sought is related to allegations of human rights violations or corruption. I urge the Court to consider the petitioner's right to information and hold the respondent liable for withholding information without a valid justification. I will demonstrate, through the course of my arguments, that the respondent's actions are in contravention of the Act, and the petitioner is entitled to the sought information.

debate_transcript.speaker: Defendant Lawyer
debate_transcript.argument:
My Lord, the impugned order dated 29th December, 2008, is based on unsubstantiated allegations of corruption, which have not been proven. The observations made by the respondent are mere assertions, lacking concrete evidence. The first proviso to Section 24(1) of the Act is not applicable in this case, as there is no conclusive finding of corruption.

The plaintiff's claims are premature, and the exceptions under Section 8(1) of the Act can be claimed, as the information sought is exempt from disclosure. The court should exercise caution in making general observations, which may prejudice the defendant's rights. The plaintiff has failed to provide sufficient evidence to substantiate their claims, and the onus of proof lies with them.

I rely on the judgment of the Hon'ble Supreme Court in the case of S.P. Gupta vs. President of India, (1981) Supp SC 87, which held that allegations must be substantiated by evidence. The plaintiff's allegations are merely speculative and do not meet the required standard of proof. Furthermore, Section 24(1) of the Act is not a blanket provision, and the exceptions under Section 8(1) must be considered. I urge the court to carefully examine the evidence and not rely on unsubstantiated allegations, ensuring that the defendant's rights are protected. The plaintiff's claims are unfounded, and I request the court to dismiss the appeal.

reasoning: The defendant has failed to provide concrete evidence to support their claim of exemption under Section 24(1) of the Act, as required by the landmark judgment of the Supreme Court in the case of R.K. Jain vs. Union of India.

The first proviso to Section 24(1) of the Act is not applicable in this case, as the allegations made by the respondent are unsubstantiated and lack merit, and there is no conclusive finding of corruption or human rights violations.

The plaintiff has a fundamental right to information under Article 19(1)(a) of the Constitution, and the exceptions under Section 8(1) of the Act must be narrowly construed, as held by the Supreme Court in the case of S.P. Gupta vs. President of India.
supporting_evidence: The judgment of the Hon'ble Supreme Court in the case of R.K. Jain vs. Union of India, which held that the Right to Information Act is a powerful tool for promoting transparency and accountability in governance.
The landmark judgment of the Supreme Court in the case of S.P. Gupta vs. President of India, (1981) Supp SC 87, which emphasizes the importance of transparency and accountability in governance.
The impugned order dated 29th December, 2008, which makes a general observation based on allegations of corruption, but fails to provide a final and determinative finding, as required by the Act.

recommendations: Provide clear and concise case facts to avoid ambiguity
Avoid using unsubstantiated allegations as a basis for judgment
Ensure that exceptions under Section 8(1) of the Act are narrowly construed and applied consistently
audit.summary: The verdict appears to be based on a sound application of legal principles, with the judge relying on relevant landmark judgments to support their decision. However, the lack of clear case facts and the use of unsubstantiated allegations as a basis for judgment may indicate some bias. Additionally, the judge's reasoning could be improved by providing more explicit explanations for the narrow construction of exceptions under Section 8(1) of the Act. No overtly biased language or discriminatory reasoning was detected.
